original_abstract:
This review provides a comprehensive evaluation of the Folin-Ciocalteu (F-C) assay, the most widely utilized method for quantifying polyphenols in natural products and dietary sources. The study details the underlying oxidation-reduction chemistry of the reagent and compares two primary analytical approaches: the traditional dual-reagent method and a streamlined single-reagent procedure. Our comparative analysis demonstrates that the single-reagent method offers superior sensitivity and precision while significantly reducing analysis time. 

The paper further addresses the critical issue of chemical interferences from non-phenolic substances such as sugars, amino acids, and proteins. We describe effective mitigation strategies, including the use of solid-phase extraction with Oasis HLB or Polyclar AT resins. Furthermore, a novel combined basic and acid hydrolysis protocol is introduced to facilitate the measurement of total polyphenols, capturing those previously sequestered in ether or ester linkages. Finally, the review explores diverse applications of the F-C assay, ranging from semi-automated microplate techniques for high-throughput screening to innovative urinary analyses used as biomarkers for dietary polyphenol intake.
humanized_abstract:
Polyphenols occur frequently in natural products and dietary components. The Folin-Ciocalteu (F-C) assay is the most frequently used method for their quantitative determination. In this review the FC assay is studied thoroughly in order to elucidate the oxidation-reduction reactions of the FC reagent and also to understand and compare the various analytical techniques employed in the assay such as dual reagent as well as single reagent methods. A detailed comparison of these two methods reveals that single reagent method is highly sensitive and selective as compared to the dual reagent method. In addition it has an advantageous shorter analytical time for determination of polyphenols.

The paper deals with some of the aspects related to the specificity of the F-C assay towards phenolic compounds. Some possible chemical interferences from non-phenolic compounds like sugars, amino acids and proteins and their possible removal through solid-phase extraction using Oasis HLB or Polyclar AT resins are dealt with in some detail. A new combined basic and acid hydrolysis procedure for the determination of total polyphenols which are believed to be retained in the ether or ester linkages is also reported. The possible applications of the F-C assay are highlighted and described in some detail. These range from the semi-automated microplate assay used for the high-throughput screening of various plant extracts to the urinary assays employed as biomarkers for dietary polyphenol intake.
